title: Gonolobus selloanus
intro: Gonolobus selloanus, also known as Sello's Gonolobus, is a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae, native to South America. This plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which may aid in the treatment of various conditions including wounds, infections, and gastrointestinal issues. The bioactive constituents of Gonolobus selloanus include alkaloids, glycosides, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Gonolobus selloanus is commonly prepared as a tincture, tea, or infusion, and is also used in the production of topical creams and ointments to promote wound healing and skin health.
taxonomy: Gonolobus selloanus is classified within the family Apocynaceae, which includes other species of flowering plants commonly known as dogbanes or oleander dock. This plant is further grouped into the subfamily Asclepiadoideae, a group of flowering plants that are often climbers or shrubs. Gonolobus selloanus is also a member of the genus Gonolobus, which consists of species of flowering plants primarily found in Central and South America. As a member of the Asclepiadoideae subfamily, Gonolobus selloanus exhibits characteristics typical of the group, such as paired leaves and inflorescences with clusters of small, fragrant flowers. Overall, the classification of Gonolobus selloanus reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary relationships within the plant kingdom.

side_effects: Gonolobus selloanus is a plant species in the Apocynaceae family, and like other plants in this family, it may have certain side effects. Gonolobus selloanus contains cardenolides, which can be toxic if ingested in large quantities. Ingestion of the plant can cause nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. There is limited research available on the specific side effects of Gonolobus selloanus, and more studies are needed to understand its potential risks.
